instruction: Plan a job queue system: decompose into components, identify risks, order dependencies, and define milestones.
output:
<think>
Planning before coding prevents the most common failure mode: discovering the wrong architecture halfway through implementation. The planning process: (1) Clarify requirements - accept jobs, persist them, distribute to workers, handle failures, report status. (2) Identify components - Job model (state machine), Queue storage (persistence layer), Worker manager (distribution + heartbeat), Retry logic (exponential backoff), Status tracker (monitoring), API layer (submission + querying). (3) Define interfaces between components so they can be developed independently. (4) Order by dependency: Job model is needed first (everything depends on it), then storage (workers need it), then worker manager, then retry logic, then API on top. (5) Identify risks: worker crashes mid-job (mitigation: heartbeat timeout returns job to PENDING), poison pills that always fail (mitigation: max retries then move to DEAD queue), exactly-once processing (hardest: use idempotency keys), queue grows unbounded (mitigation: backpressure via bounded queue). (6) Define milestones: M1 can enqueue/dequeue, M2 worker processes end-to-end, M3 failed job retries, M4 HTTP API works.
</think>

instruction: Plan a database migration with incremental steps, rollback scenarios, and the expand-then-contract pattern.
output:
<think>
Database migration is one of the highest-risk operations in production. A bad migration causes downtime, data loss, or corruption. Planning is essential. The process: (1) Document current schema exactly. (2) Design target schema. (3) Compute the diff - what needs to change? (4) Plan incremental steps - each step must be small and independently reversible. (5) Never combine schema change with data migration in one step. (6) The expand-then-contract pattern: first ADD new columns/tables (expand phase - safe, no existing data affected). Deploy code that writes to both old and new schemas. Migrate historical data. Switch reads to new schema. Finally DROP old columns (contract phase). Each phase has its own rollback SQL. (7) Test on staging first with production-size data. (8) Risk assessment per step: adding nullable column is LOW risk, backfilling data is MEDIUM, adding NOT NULL constraint is MEDIUM (must verify no nulls first), dropping column is HIGH (irreversible data loss).
</think>

instruction: Plan feature implementation for parallel development by defining interfaces and stubs first.
output:
<think>
Interface-first planning is the key to parallel development. When you define the contracts between layers upfront, multiple teams can work simultaneously without blocking each other. The process: (1) Define the data model that all teams share (Notification, Channel, etc). (2) Define Protocol/Interface classes for each component (NotificationRepository, DeliveryService, UserPreferences). Each protocol specifies exactly what methods exist, what they accept, and what they return. (3) Create stub implementations that satisfy the interfaces with minimal code (in-memory dict for repository, print statement for delivery). (4) The core service is written against the protocols, not concrete implementations. It works immediately with stubs. (5) Now teams work in parallel: Team A implements real PostgreSQL repository, Team B builds email/push delivery channels, Team C builds user preference management, Team D builds API endpoints using stubs. (6) Integration happens last when all pieces are ready, and swapping stubs for real implementations is a one-line change. This approach reduces total calendar time significantly.
</think>

instruction: Plan a caching layer: analyze access patterns, choose strategy, plan invalidation, and handle cache failures.
output:
<think>
Cache planning requires analyzing access patterns before choosing a strategy. Wrong caching decisions cause stale data, inconsistency, or paradoxically slower performance. Analysis steps: (1) Read/write ratio - high read ratio (>10:1) strongly favors caching. (2) Data freshness requirements - real-time data cannot be cached; config data can be cached for minutes. (3) Working set size - if hot data fits in RAM, caching is highly effective. (4) Access distribution - Zipf distribution (few hot keys) means caching helps a lot. Uniform means less benefit. Strategy selection: Cache-aside (lazy loading) is simplest - app checks cache, on miss loads from DB, populates cache. Write-through ensures consistency - every write goes to cache AND DB synchronously. Write-behind (write-back) maximizes write performance but risks data loss if cache crashes before async DB write. Invalidation: TTL-based (simple, eventual consistency), event-based (on DB update, invalidate cache), version-based (increment version counter). Failure handling: when cache is down, the system MUST still work by falling through to the database with higher latency. Never let cache failure cause total failure.
</think>
